title: AMI IN1001 video shot detector technical review
expected_context: The meeting is a technical status and demo review for a video shot detector.
The goal is to present and explain the video shot detector and review results on CINETIS video data.
The discussion covers shot detection, keyframe extraction, motion features, histogram distance, Bhattacharya distance, MMM data handling, privacy, and code/library usage.
expected_decisions: Use shot-level segmentation as useful output for CINETIS-style restoration or color-correction workflows.
Use keyframes and shot summaries as a practical way for users to inspect video content.
Treat motion features as useful for difficult transitions, while recognizing that histogram-based methods can handle many simple cuts.
Use the distance output file to retune or reprocess shot thresholds without reprocessing the full video when possible.
Keep the frame step parameter at one for shot detection to avoid losing temporal precision.
Leave the sub-threshold parameter at one.
Use OpenCV or the MPEG input reader path rather than relying on the old tosh library.
expected_requirements: The detector should output XML describing detected shots.
The detector should output a distance file containing distances between histograms.
The system should support keyframe extraction.
The MMM data workflow should allow videos and XML output to be placed in a data directory for browsing.
The browser or interface should be able to show detected shots and associated frames.
Privacy must be considered when publishing or browsing videos through FTP, HTTP, or MMM web paths.
Directories exposed through the browser should not be publicly listable when privacy is required.
expected_technical_findings: The demo video detector found about thirty shots in the reviewed CINETIS example.
Each shot can be expanded to show keyframes.
The current keyframes are extracted by downsampling, not by using sub-shots.
Real Media compression reduces visual quality for the demo video.
Dissolves are harder to detect than simple cuts.
Histogram distance can detect many changes, but motion features can better explain difficult transitions.
Black or low-texture regions are poor for motion estimation.
Shot detection latency is related to about half of the sliding window.
The alpha threshold discussed is around 2.4.
Some code was originally tied to older libraries, but OpenCV and related image-processing classes can be used instead.

expected_actions.action: Provide the shot detector parameter or configuration file discussed at the end of the meeting.
Help review the C code and video-structure classes if newer versions of the detector are used.
Explain or document how to put video data and XML output into the MMM data workflow.
Check whether the DVD/video grabbing issue produced an incorrect or duplicated video file.
Take privacy/password protection into account before exposing experiment videos or MMM browser directories.

expected_risks: Compressed or low-quality video can make visual review harder.
Dissolves and ambiguous transitions may not be detected cleanly.
Low-texture or black frames reduce the usefulness of motion estimation.
Some libraries or older code may not compile cleanly on Debian.
Incorrect video grabbing or cached frames can lead to wrong browser output.
Publicly accessible MMM or browser directories can create privacy issues.
expected_open_questions: Whether certain ambiguous transitions should be labelled as shot changes.
Whether the CINETIS workflow needs better keyframe extraction or a more detailed summary.
Whether stabilization tasks require a different segmentation than color correction or restoration tasks.
How much manual labelling the client or user should do.
Whether a simple user-facing tool should let users select shots, assign names, and create chapters.
How best to handle cache invalidation when videos are corrected or re-grabbed.
Which parts of the existing code should be kept, updated, or replaced with OpenCV-based components.
acceptable_behavior: Summarize this as a technical demo and review of video shot detection.
Capture shot detection, keyframes, motion features, histogram distance, MMM workflow, code-library issues, and privacy concerns.
Treat the detector and workflow as technical/research infrastructure rather than a customer-facing business launch.
Include explicit technical follow-ups without inventing deadlines.
Leave business decisions and commercial launch risks empty or none-equivalent.
must_not_invent: A final decision that all CINETIS videos are ready
Named people beyond Speaker A, Speaker B, Speaker C, and Speaker D
Facts from outside the first 50-minute clip
